Gene_Accession_Number.reason: The Results state the 1.6 kb DNA fragment containing Cfla-Avr4 'has been deposited into GenBank under accession number MH673051'.

Source_Species.reason: The gene cloned and studied is explicitly from Cercospora cf. flagellaris.

Source_Species_Latin_Name.reason: The paper consistently names the organism as Cercospora cf. flagellaris.

Source_Species_Variety.reason: The Experimental Procedures identify the wild-type isolate used for cloning/disruption as Cercospora cf. flagellaris isolate MRL 6020-2B.

Applied_Species.reason: All functional tests were performed in Cercospora cf. flagellaris.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: The applied/tested species in mutants and dsRNA assays is Cercospora cf. flagellaris.

Applied_Species_Variety.reason: The disruption mutants were generated from wild-type isolate MRL 6020-2B, and dsRNA work used the wild type C. cf. flagellaris isolate from the study.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: The paper directly tests Avr4 effects on cercosporin production.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: Cercosporin is the metabolite studied, but the exact label 'perylenequinone toxin' does not appear in the paper text provided.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: In Discussion, cercosporin is explicitly described as a virulence factor and linked to membrane breakdown, cell death, and disease development; Results also connect reduced cercosporin with reduced lesions/virulence.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: Results show Avr4 disruption mutants and Avr4 dsRNA treatment both greatly reduce cercosporin production, lower CTB1/CTB8 expression, and Avr4 mutants show reduced virulence on soybean.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: All listed methods are reported: targeted gene disruption, dsRNA-mediated suppression, RT-qPCR of Avr4/CTB1/CTB8, cercosporin quantification by absorbance and HPLC, detached leaf virulence assay, fungal biomass qPCR, and chitinase sensitivity assay.

Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species.reason: Avr4 expression was measured in wild type in dsRNA experiments and was 'suppressed by 78%' after Avr4-specific dsRNA treatment.

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: Reduced expression after Avr4 dsRNA treatment is supported, but 'absent in avr4 mutants' was not directly shown by expression analysis in the paper.

Subcellular_Localization.reason: The paper discusses AVR4 as an effector and cites prior studies showing AVR4 presence in the apoplast, while not determining localization here; the statement itself notes exact localization in this paper is NA.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: Sequence analysis identified AVR4 as a chitin-binding protein with a peritrophin-A domain (pfam01607), and the protein is described as cysteine-rich/conserved with nine cysteines.

Interacting_Proteins.reason: The paper repeatedly describes AVR4 as a chitin-binding protein; thus interaction with chitin is supported.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: The study explicitly examined light/dark conditions for CTB expression, chitinase exposure, and Avr4-specific dsRNA treatment.

Potential_Tradeoffs.reason: Results show avr4 mutants grew faster on PDA, but were more chitinase-sensitive and less virulent on soybean leaves.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: Each numeric statement matches Results: ~10% cercosporin on PDA, 3-7% in liquid, CTB1/CTB8 reduced about two- to three-fold, ~50% lower fungal growth in leaves, Avr4 reduced 78% by dsRNA, cercosporin reduced 88%, and CTB1/CTB8 also reduced.

Other_Important_Info.reason: The paper's main focus is Avr4; it notes multiple HYG copies in mutants and uses independent dsRNA silencing to confirm the phenotype. It also presents AVR4 as an effector/chitin-binding protein affecting pathway gene expression rather than a pathway enzyme.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: The data support Avr4 as a possible RNA-based control target, but this application value is an extrapolation and is not explicitly presented in the paper.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: Cercosporin was quantified from fungal cultures/colonies and from infected soybean leaves.

Source_Species.reason: CTB1 expression analyzed is from Cercospora cf. flagellaris.

Source_Species_Latin_Name.reason: The species studied for CTB1 expression is explicitly C. cf. flagellaris.

Source_Species_Variety.reason: Expression assays used wild-type isolate MRL 6020-2B and its avr4-derived mutants.

Applied_Species.reason: The tested system for CTB1 expression is C. cf. flagellaris.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: CTB1 was measured in C. cf. flagellaris cultures.

Applied_Species_Variety.reason: The applied isolate background is the wild-type MRL 6020-2B and its derived mutants/treatments.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: CTB1 is explicitly treated as a cercosporin biosynthesis pathway gene, and its expression changes are linked to cercosporin production.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: The paper studies cercosporin, but the exact term 'perylenequinone toxin' is not stated in the provided text.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: Cercosporin is discussed as a virulence factor contributing to disease development and host damage.

Biosynthetic_Pathway.reason: CTB1 is directly called one of the 'cercosporin biosynthesis pathway genes'.

Enzyme_Name.reason: The paper names the gene CTB1 but does not explicitly expand it here as 'cercosporin toxin biosynthesis protein CTB1'.

Catalyzed_Reaction_Description.reason: No specific catalytic role or polyketide synthase step for CTB1 is described in this paper.

Metabolic_Step_Position.reason: The paper does not assign CTB1 an 'upstream' step position.

End_Product_Connection_Type.reason: Because CTB1 is explicitly a cercosporin biosynthesis pathway gene and its lowered expression tracks with reduced cercosporin, connection to biosynthesis of the target product is supported.

Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step.reason: This field states 'not established in this paper,' which is consistent with the paper not making such a claim.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: Results show lower CTB1 expression in avr4 mutants and after Avr4 dsRNA treatment alongside strongly reduced cercosporin accumulation.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: The study used real-time RT-PCR for CTB1 expression in avr4 mutants and dsRNA-treated wild type, together with cercosporin quantification by absorbance/HPLC.

Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species.reason: Figure 3/Results state CTB1 expression was significantly reduced in avr4 mutants under both light and dark conditions.

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: CTB1 was reduced in avr4 mutants and also significantly reduced (75%) after Avr4 dsRNA treatment.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: The paper does not state that CTB1 is a polyketide synthase or give a domain/family annotation.

Interacting_Proteins.reason: No physical interaction between CTB1 and AVR4 is shown or claimed; only expression correlation is reported.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: CTB1 expression was evaluated under light versus dark conditions and after Avr4-targeting dsRNA treatment.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: The summary matches Results: CTB1 reduced about two- to three-fold in avr4 mutants, reduced about 75% after Avr4 dsRNA treatment, and this correlated with major loss of cercosporin.

Other_Important_Info.reason: CTB1 was indeed monitored as a pathway readout and was not directly mutated/manipulated in this paper.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: Cercosporin accumulation was measured in fungal culture and infected leaves, but this field is attached to CTB1 rather than directly demonstrated as a CTB1-specific site statement.
